title: Listening - Part 1
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two managers talking about interviewing people for jobs.
extracts.audio_script:
Man: Interviewing can be tricky, can’t it?
Woman: Yes, it can throw up unexpected issues like even if a candidate is new to the particular role, [1]they need to demonstrate that they’re a self-starter and have got a bit about them.[/1] I also think it tells you a lot about how well someone will perform on any job. This doesn’t always come across, of course.
Man: It helps if a candidate has a proven track record, obviously, but [1]the ability to come up with their own solutions and ideas is what should shine through at interview.[/1] I know people keep banging on about the importance of expressing yourself effectively but frankly anyone can learn that.
Woman: And I don’t want to hire someone who isn’t going to be happy with the job they’re getting. I had one candidate – extremely bright, very motivated, but [2]didn’t ask a single question about the work itself, just about how quickly she could get promotion.[/2] She’d obviously done her homework on what sort of company we are and how fast we’re expanding. Interestingly, she did well on the group tasks so that wasn’t the issue.
Man: Yes, I always look for someone who shows they can get on with colleagues.

questions.question: The man and the woman both say that job candidates should display
questions.explanation: The woman mentions needing to be a 'self-starter' and the man mentions the 'ability to come up with their own solutions', both of which are synonyms for initiative.

questions.question: The woman says that a person she recently interviewed

options.A: did insufficient research about the company.
options.B: failed to demonstrate an ability to work in a team.
options.C: revealed her lack of interest in the post she was applying for.

questions.explanation: By not asking about the work itself and only about promotion, the candidate showed she wasn't interested in the actual job duties of the post.

extracts.context: You hear two friends discussing online learning compared to college-based learning.
extracts.audio_script:
Man: I’m thinking of taking a computer course to improve my IT skills for work, but I can’t decide between going to college or an online course? Any ideas?
Woman: [3]It sounds like you’re looking for practical knowledge. That’s something a lot of online courses excel at. They’re actually designed to prepare you for work so there’s less focus on theory. Also, I reckon you’ll find online wins on up-to-date content.[/3] It doesn’t have to be planned well in advance like college courses do. For something like language courses though, I’d opt for college every time, just for the face-to-face interaction, but good online courses will still have a trainer available by phone if you need help.
Man: Are there any drawbacks with online learning do you think?
Woman: If you want a list, just think about the pros of traditional education. Online’s great provided you can stay motivated. You know, [4]it can be tempting to just check one more email, or do a bit of surfing![/4] And students don’t get the same networking opportunities, or the chances to meet other students that they do in college. Plus, a lot of online qualifications aren’t that widely recognised by employers. You just want to brush up your skills, though, so that shouldn’t matter.

questions.question: Why does the woman think an online course would be better than a college course for the man?

options.A: It would be more likely to meet his particular needs.
options.B: He would receive more personal attention.
options.C: It would be easier to prepare for.

questions.explanation: She explains that online courses are better for 'practical knowledge' and 'up-to-date content', which matches his goal of improving IT skills for work.

questions.question: The woman warns the man that if he does an online course, he might

options.A: have problems focussing on his studies.
options.B: need to cut back on his social life.

questions.explanation: The temptation to 'check email' or 'surf' the web instead of studying refers to a difficulty in maintaining focus.

extracts.context: You hear two friends talking about the jetlag they suffer after long aeroplane flights.
extracts.audio_script:
Woman: Hi Jon! You’re just back from your business trip, aren’t you?
Man: Yeah – and still getting over the jetlag from a 14-hour flight. Colleagues seem shocked at my confusion when they ask me about sales figures, ’cos I usually have no trouble bringing this sort of stuff immediately to mind.
Woman: I see.
Man: And as for this month’s reports for the executive board – [5]good job they’ve extended the deadline when they heard what’s been happening to me[/5], as I can’t even get my head around the thought of them! Routine work is less challenging though, I can just plod through the humdrum stuff at my own pace, trying to force my brain back into gear.
Woman: Well, although [6]hopping into bed for twelve hours’ sleep... is actually the worst thing[/6] – the body clock just gets confused. [6]The quickest way to feel better is just to have short catnaps. You can also lessen the impact... by keeping yourself hydrated... and then exposing yourself to lots of sunlight[/6] when you arrive.
Man: Mmm… interesting.

questions.question: How does the man feel when he has jetlag?

options.A: surprised by his inability to remember things
options.B: frustrated with the monotony of everyday tasks
options.C: relieved at the understanding shown by superiors

questions.explanation: He says 'good job' they extended the deadline, which expresses relief that his bosses were understanding of his situation.

questions.question: What is the woman doing when talking to her friend about jetlag?
questions.explanation: She evaluates different options, calling one 'the worst thing' and suggesting others like catnaps, hydration, and sunlight as better alternatives.
